model_narrative: We evaluate the smaller of a single real argument and zero by writing it as a mixed-integer program. The argument is free to take any value within a fixed lower and upper bound. We split the argument into a nonnegative positive part and a nonnegative negative part, and we use a binary indicator to decide which of the two parts is active. The result variable records the value of the function. The objective is to minimize the result.

params.name: xlo
params.doc: the lower bound on the argument, a scalar equal to -5; its absolute value serves as the large constant that switches off the negative part when the indicator is on

params.name: xup
params.doc: the upper bound on the argument, a scalar equal to 5; its absolute value serves as the large constant that switches off the positive part when the indicator is off

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the argument to the function, a free real variable bounded between the lower and upper bounds

vars.name: xp
vars.doc: the positive part of the argument, a nonnegative real that captures the argument when it is at or above zero

vars.name: xn
vars.doc: the negative part of the argument, a nonnegative real that captures the magnitude of the argument when it is below zero

vars.name: b
vars.doc: a binary indicator that equals 1 when the argument is represented by its positive part and 0 when it is represented by its negative part

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the result of the function evaluation, a free real variable holding the minimum of the argument and zero

constraints.name: e1
constraints.intent: The argument must equal its positive part minus its negative part, so the two parts together reconstruct the original argument.

constraints.name: e2
constraints.intent: The positive part may only be nonzero when the indicator is on. When the indicator is on the positive part can range up to the magnitude of the upper bound, and when the indicator is off the positive part is forced to zero.

constraints.name: e3
constraints.intent: The negative part may only be nonzero when the indicator is off. When the indicator is off the negative part can range up to the magnitude of the lower bound, and when the indicator is on the negative part is forced to zero.

constraints.name: defzmin
constraints.intent: The result must equal the negation of the negative part, so the result is zero when the argument is nonnegative and equals the argument itself when the argument is below zero.

query: There's a proposal on the table to put a single budget on the position's total swing — the gross of its up-leg and its down-leg together shouldn't run past 3 units of exposure in one period. What downside does the optimizer report once that combined exposure budget is in place?

query: Risk is pitching a symmetry rule that ties the two legs together: the down-leg exposure shouldn't be allowed to exceed the up-leg by more than 4 units. Tie the negative part to the positive part that way and walk me through where the downside lands.

query: We're looking at a mandate from the desk that whenever the position sits on its short/down side, it has to carry a full up-leg allocation of 5 instead — effectively the branch flag has to flip to its positive setting before any down-leg is allowed. Wire that linkage between the up-leg and the branch indicator and tell me what y comes back as.

query: The plan keeps booking the deepest possible down-leg even though the branch indicator is sitting idle. Shouldn't a deep down-leg only be on the table once that positive-branch flag is actually switched on — say, capping the down-leg at the flag's setting scaled to 5, plus a small standing allowance of 2 when the flag is off? What's the model seeing that lets it run the down-leg out so far without engaging the flag?

query: The down-leg runs out far ahead of anything carried on the up-leg, which looks lopsided. Wouldn't it be more balanced to make twice the down-leg stay within the up-leg plus a fixed slack of 2 — so the short side can't keep dwarfing the long side? What's pulling the optimizer toward that imbalance?
